content:
You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

# Phase 1: Analyze the Fix

This is a patch for CorrelatedValuePropagation:

Reland "[CVP] Check whether the default case is reachable (#79993)" (#96089)
Reland "[CVP] Check whether the default case is reachable (#79993)" (#96089)

This patch reverts https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/81585 as
https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/78582 has been landed.
Now clang works well with reproducer
https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/79993#issuecomment-1936822679.
commit 394e321a2ea021194891cf5b0c5747a901fb7915
Author: Yingwei Zheng <dtcxzyw2333@gmail.com>
Date:   Thu Jun 20 13:45:18 2024 +0800

    Reland "[CVP] Check whether the default case is reachable (#79993)" (#96089)
    
    This patch reverts https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/81585 as
    https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/78582 has been landed.
    Now clang works well with reproducer
    https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/pull/79993#issuecomment-1936822679.

diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Scalar/CorrelatedValuePropagation.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Scalar/CorrelatedValuePropagation.cpp
index 50b5fdb56720..88adeb597e75 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Scalar/CorrelatedValuePropagation.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Scalar/CorrelatedValuePropagation.cpp
@@ -366,6 +366,7 @@ static bool processSwitch(SwitchInst *I, LazyValueInfo *LVI,
   { // Scope for SwitchInstProfUpdateWrapper. It must not live during
     // ConstantFoldTerminator() as the underlying SwitchInst can be changed.
     SwitchInstProfUpdateWrapper SI(*I);
+    unsigned ReachableCaseCount = 0;
 
     for (auto CI = SI->case_begin(), CE = SI->case_end(); CI != CE;) {
       ConstantInt *Case = CI->getCaseValue();
@@ -402,6 +403,31 @@ static bool processSwitch(SwitchInst *I, LazyValueInfo *LVI,
 
       // Increment the case iterator since we didn't delete it.
       ++CI;
+      ++ReachableCaseCount;
+    }
+
+    BasicBlock *DefaultDest = SI->getDefaultDest();
+    if (ReachableCaseCount > 1 &&
+        !isa<UnreachableInst>(DefaultDest->getFirstNonPHIOrDbg())) {
+      ConstantRange CR = LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse(I->getOperandUse(0),
+                                                    /*UndefAllowed*/ false);
+      // The default dest is unreachable if all cases are covered.
+      if (!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)) {
+        BasicBlock *NewUnreachableBB =
+            BasicBlock::Create(BB->getContext(), "default.unreachable",
+                               BB->getParent(), DefaultDest);
+        new UnreachableInst(BB->getContext(), NewUnreachableBB);
+
+        DefaultDest->removePredecessor(BB);
+        SI->setDefaultDest(NewUnreachableBB);
+
+        if (SuccessorsCount[DefaultDest] == 1)
+          DTU.applyUpdates({{DominatorTree::Delete, BB, DefaultDest}});
+        DTU.applyUpdates({{DominatorTree::Insert, BB, NewUnreachableBB}});
+
+        ++NumDeadCases;
+        Changed = true;
+      }
     }
   }
 
@@ -1283,6 +1309,12 @@ CorrelatedValuePropagationPass::run(Function &F, FunctionAnalysisManager &AM) {
   if (!Changed) {
     PA = PreservedAnalyses::all();
   } else {
+#if defined(EXPENSIVE_CHECKS)
+    assert(DT->verify(DominatorTree::VerificationLevel::Full));
+#else
+    assert(DT->verify(DominatorTree::VerificationLevel::Fast));
+#endif // EXPENSIVE_CHECKS
+
     PA.preserve<DominatorTreeAnalysis>();
     PA.preserve<LazyValueAnalysis>();
   }


---

## Subsystem Knowledge for CorrelatedValuePropagation

For reference, here are some key points about the CorrelatedValuePropagation you should consider (but do not limit to these) in your analysis:

No specific knowledge provided for this component.

**CRITICAL**: Subsystem knowledge is **ONLY** a reference for you to understand this component.
Do NOT start your analysis from these patterns. Start from the patch semantics. Use these **ONLY** to cross-check your reasoning.

## Your Task for Phase 1

In this phase, you will review and analyze the fix above carefully and identify potential issues, edge cases, or gaps that the fix might miss. According to the analysis, propose mutation strategies for exposing the issue. These strategies will be used in Phase 2 to mutate existing test cases to target the identified issues.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Context Gathering
In this step, you should gather enough context to construct semantics model of this fix for future analysis. Your goal is to understand the complete flow of this optimization. To achieve this, you should look at the nearby code regions, or try to find similar code regions in the LLVM codebase to understand the semantics of this fix.

You can use search tools:
- Use the `find` and `list` tools to find relevant files in the LLVM code.
- Use the `read` and `grep` tools to understand the context of the fix.

### Step 2: Fix Segmentation and Analysis
Based on the model built for this fix, you should segment the fix into smaller parts and analyze the semantics of each part. Follow this structured workflow for segmentation and analysis:

- **Segmentation**: Break down the fix into smaller segments or key changes, especially focusing on code regions related to semantics changes.
- **Analysis**: For each segment, analyze the semantics and identify any assumptions or preconditions that the fix relies on.

#### Tips for this step
- You can use the `langref` tool to query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- You can pay attention to the commit message and annotations in the code, such as comments or specific patterns that indicate important semantics or assumptions.

### Step 3: Formal Semantic Modeling
- Define the exact semantic contract that this fix claims to satisfy.
- Explicitly list the preconditions under which the contract is assumed to hold.
- Identify how these preconditions may be violated.

### Step 4: Identify Potential Issues and Propose Test Strategies
Based on your analysis, propose around 5 specific test strategies that can be used to expose the issue. When proposing test strategies, consider the following:
- **Focus**: Focus on the assumptions and preconditions you identified.
- **Scenarios**: Think about specific input characteristics or scenarios that could violate these assumptions.
- **Mutations**: Propose mutations that can be applied to existing test cases to create new test cases targeting these scenarios.

Once you have analyzed the fix and identified potential issues or edge cases, use the `stop` tool to specify proposed test strategies along with detailed reasoning. 
Follow this structure for reasoning:

### 1. Fix Understanding
- What optimization or transformation does this fix enable or correct?
- What are the key changes made in the patch?

### 2. Formal Semantic Modeling
- What is the exact semantic function before the fix?
- What is the exact semantic function after the fix?
- Under what conditions might they differ?
- Provide at least one concrete numerical instantiation to illustrate the difference.

### 3. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified
- What assumptions does the fix make about the input?
- What preconditions must hold for this fix to be correct?

### 4. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions
For each assumption and precondition identified, describe specific scenarios or input characteristics that could violate them. The potential cases should only focus on the semantics related to the analysis above.

### 5. Test Strategies
For each potential issue you identified, propose specific test strategies with the following structure:

- Name: [Name]
- Target: [What to mutate]
- Rationale: [Why this might expose an issue]
- Expected Issue: [What incorrect behavior might occur]

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `langref`: Query the LLVM Language Reference Manual for specific instructions, semantics, or optimization details relevant to the fix.
- `stop`: End Phase 1 by submitting the identified issues and proposed test strategies.

name: langref
arguments: {"inst":"switch"}

output:
.. _i_switch:

'``switch``' Instruction
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^

Syntax:
"""""""

::

      switch <intty> <value>, label <defaultdest> [ <intty> <val>, label <dest> ... ]

Overview:
"""""""""

The '``switch``' instruction is used to transfer control flow to one of
several different places. It is a generalization of the '``br``'
instruction, allowing a branch to occur to one of many possible
destinations.

Arguments:
""""""""""

The '``switch``' instruction uses three parameters: an integer
comparison value '``value``', a default '``label``' destination, and an
array of pairs of comparison value constants and '``label``'s. The table
is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries.

Semantics:
""""""""""

The ``switch`` instruction specifies a table of values and destinations.
When the '``switch``' instruction is executed, this table is searched
for the given value. If the value is found, control flow is transferred
to the corresponding destination; otherwise, control flow is transferred
to the default destination.
If '``value``' is ``poison`` or ``undef``, this instruction has undefined
behavior.

Implementation:
"""""""""""""""

Depending on properties of the target machine and the particular
``switch`` instruction, this instruction may be code generated in
different ways. For example, it could be generated as a series of
chained conditional branches or with a lookup table.

Example:
""""""""

.. code-block:: llvm

     ; Emulate a conditional br instruction
     %Val = zext i1 %value to i32
     switch i32 %Val, label %truedest [ i32 0, label %falsedest ]

     ; Emulate an unconditional br instruction
     switch i32 0, label %dest [ ]

     ; Implement a jump table:
     switch i32 %val, label %otherwise [ i32 0, label %onzero
                                         i32 1, label %onone
                                         i32 2, label %ontwo ]

arguments:
{"thoughts":"CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Use specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

1.  **Fix Understanding**:
    The patch aims to optimize `switch` instructions by checking if the default case is reachable. It does this by counting the number of reachable cases (`ReachableCaseCount`). If the size of the constant range of the switch condition (`CR`) is less than or equal to `ReachableCaseCount`, it assumes that all possible values in the constant range are covered by the cases, and therefore the default case is unreachable. It then replaces the default destination with an `unreachable` instruction.

2.  **Formal Semantic Modeling**:
    *   **Before**: The default case is left as is, unless all cases are explicitly handled or other optimizations apply.
    *   **After**: If `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)`, the default case is replaced with an `unreachable` block.
    *   **Condition for difference**: This differs when `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)` is true, but the default case is actually reachable.
    *   **Concrete instantiation**: Suppose the switch condition `x` has a constant range `CR = [0, 2)` (size 2, containing 0 and 1). The switch has cases `0` and `2`.
        If `LVI->getPredicateAt(x == 2)` returns `Unknown` (meaning it fails to prove it's false), the case `2` is kept.
        `ReachableCaseCount` becomes 2 (for cases `0` and `2`).
        `CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is `false` (since size is 2, which is not larger than 2).
        The optimization triggers and marks the default case as unreachable.
        However, if `x` is `1` (which is in `CR`), it should go to the default case. But the default case is now `unreachable`, causing undefined behavior!

3.  **Assumptions Identified**:
    *   The patch assumes that if `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getSize()`, then the reachable cases *must* cover all values in `CR`.
    *   This assumption relies on the precondition that *all* reachable cases are strictly within `CR`. If there are cases outside `CR` that were not eliminated (because `LVI->getPredicateAt` was not powerful enough to prove them false, or due to LVI cache inconsistencies), they will artificially inflate `ReachableCaseCount`.

4.  **Potential Cases to Break Assumptions**:
    *   We need a scenario where `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` is more precise than `LVI->getPredicateAt`, or where they yield different results such that a case outside the range is not eliminated.
    *   For example, `getConstantRangeAtUse` might use context-sensitive information (like `UseBlockValue=true` equivalent, though it doesn't take a block, it takes a Use). Wait, `getConstantRangeAtUse` takes a `Use`, which provides context. `getPredicateAt` takes a context instruction `I` and `UseBlockValue=true`.
    *   What if `CR` is calculated using some range metadata or assume that `getPredicateAt` misses?
    *   Or what if `CR` is `[0, 2)` and cases are `0` and `2`. If `getPredicateAt` for `x == 2` returns `Unknown`.
    *   Actually, `LVI` is known to sometimes have inconsistencies between `getPredicateAt` and `getConstantRange`.
    *   Another way: `ReachableCaseCount` counts the number of cases. What if there are multiple cases with the same value? LangRef says "The table is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries." So this is not possible.
    *   What if the switch condition is a `select` or `phi` where `getConstantRangeAtUse` can bound it, but `getPredicateAt` fails?
    *   Let's construct a test strategy based on this discrepancy. If we can make `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getSize()` while the cases do not cover `CR`, we expose the bug.

5.  **Test Strategies**:
    *   Strategy 1: Create a switch with a condition that has a known small range (e.g., `[0, 2)`). Add cases `0` and `2`. Obfuscate the condition so `getPredicateAt` cannot prove `cond == 2` is false, but `getConstantRangeAtUse` can still determine the range is `[0, 2)`.
    *   Strategy 2: Use `assume` to restrict the range of a value to `[0, 2)`. Switch on this value with cases `0` and `2`.
    *   Strategy 3: Use a `phi` node where one incoming value is `0` and the other is `1`. Switch on the `phi` with cases `0` and `2`.
    *   Strategy 4: Use `and x, 1` to restrict range to `[0, 2)`. Switch on it with cases `0` and `2`. (Though `getPredicateAt` might easily fold this).
    *   Strategy 5: Use `urem x, 2` to restrict range to `[0, 2)`. Switch on it with cases `0` and `2`.

    Let's refine Strategy 1/2/3. The core issue is the logical flaw: `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getSize()` does NOT imply `CR` is fully covered, unless we are guaranteed that all `ReachableCaseCount` cases are *inside* `CR`. Since `getPredicateAt` and `getConstantRangeAtUse` are different API calls, they might have different precision, leading to cases outside `CR` being counted. This is a classic LLVM pass bug where two different analysis queries are assumed to be perfectly consistent.","strategies":[{"rationale":"The patch checks if `ReachableCaseCount > 1` and if the constant range size is not larger than `ReachableCaseCount`. If so, it replaces the default destination with an unreachable block. However, if there are multiple reachable cases but they do not cover the entire constant range, the default case might still be reachable. The condition `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)` might be satisfied if the constant range size is exactly equal to `ReachableCaseCount`, but the cases might not exactly match the values in the constant range. Wait, `CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)` checks if the number of elements in the constant range is strictly greater than `ReachableCaseCount`. If it's not, it means the number of elements in the constant range is less than or equal to `ReachableCaseCount`. If the constant range has `N` elements and there are `M` reachable cases, and `N <= M`, does it mean all elements in the constant range are covered by the cases? Not necessarily. What if multiple cases have the same value? The LLVM Language Reference says "The table is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries." So all cases have distinct values. If the constant range has `N` elements and there are `M` distinct reachable cases, and `N <= M`, and all cases are within the constant range? Wait, the cases might not be within the constant range. If a case is outside the constant range, `LVI->getPredicateAt` would return `LazyValueInfo::False` and the case would be removed. So all remaining cases must overlap with the constant range. Since they are distinct integers, and there are `M` of them, and the constant range only contains `N` integers, and `N <= M`, it must be that `N = M` and the cases exactly cover the constant range. Wait, what if the constant range is not a single contiguous range, but `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` returns a bounding range? If the bounding range has size `N`, and there are `M` cases, `N <= M` implies the bounding range is exactly covered.
Wait, what if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are NOT in the constant range?
Let's trace:
```cpp
    for (auto CI = SI->case_begin(), CE = SI->case_end(); CI != CE;) {
      ConstantInt *Case = CI->getCaseValue();
      LazyValueInfo::Tristate State =
          LVI->getPredicateAt(CmpInst::ICMP_EQ, Cond, Case, I,
                              /* UseBlockValue */ true);

      if (State == LazyValueInfo::False) {
        // removed
        continue;
      }
      if (State == LazyValueInfo::True) {
        // replaced with unconditional branch
        break;
      }
      ++CI;
      ++ReachableCaseCount;
    }
```
If `State` is `LazyValueInfo::Unknown`, the case is kept and `ReachableCaseCount` is incremented.
Could there be a case where `State` is `Unknown` but the case value is actually outside the constant range?
`LVI->getPredicateAt` might return `Unknown` even if the value is outside the constant range returned by `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse`, because they might use different analysis depths or context. However, usually `getPredicateAt` is at least as precise as `getConstantRangeAtUse`.
But what if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are unreachable, but LVI couldn't prove it? Then `ReachableCaseCount` could be larger than the actual number of reachable cases.
If `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getUpper() - CR.getLower()`, the patch assumes the default case is unreachable.
Is it possible that `ReachableCaseCount` is large because of some cases outside the constant range, and the cases INSIDE the constant range do NOT cover the whole constant range?
Yes! If `LVI->getPredicateAt` returns `Unknown` for a case outside the constant range (e.g., due to LVI limitations or different context), that case is counted in `ReachableCaseCount`. Then `ReachableCaseCount` could be >= `CR.getSetSize()`. But the cases inside `CR` might not cover all values in `CR`. Thus, there is a value in `CR` that is not covered by any case, so the default case is reachable. But the patch replaces it with `unreachable`!","target":"A switch instruction where the condition has a known constant range, but there are many cases, some of which are outside the constant range but LVI fails to prove them false, and the cases inside the constant range do not cover the entire constant range.","expected_issue":"The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior when the default case is actually reachable.","name":"Reachable Default Case with Multiple Reachable Cases"},{"rationale":"The patch uses `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse(I->getOperandUse(0), /*UndefAllowed*/ false)`. If the switch condition can be `undef` or `poison`, the constant range might not include `undef` or `poison`. Wait, if the condition is `undef` or `poison`, the `switch` instruction has undefined behavior anyway (as per LangRef: "If 'value' is poison or undef, this instruction has undefined behavior."). So replacing the default dest with `unreachable` is valid if the only way to reach it is if the condition is `undef`/`poison`.","target":"Switch instruction with undef/poison condition.","expected_issue":"The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior.","name":"Constant Range with Undef/Poison"},{"name":"Switch Condition is a Select with Unreachable Cases","expected_issue":"The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior.","rationale":"Consider a switch condition `cond = select i1 %cmp, i32 %a, i32 %b`. If the constant range of `cond` is small, but there are many cases. Wait, the issue is whether `ReachableCaseCount` can be larger than the constant range size, while the cases don't cover the constant range.
Let's construct a case where `LVI->getPredicateAt` returns `Unknown` for a case outside the constant range, but `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` returns a small range.
`LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` uses `ValueLatticeElement`.
Is it possible that `CR` is small, but `ReachableCaseCount` is large?
Suppose `cond` is `and i32 %x, 3`. The constant range is `[0, 4)`. Size is 4.
Suppose the switch has cases: 0, 1, 4, 5, 6.
Will `LVI->getPredicateAt(ICMP_EQ, cond, 4)` return `False`?
`cond` is `and i32 %x, 3`. LVI knows its range is `[0, 4)`. So `cond == 4` is `False`.
What if `cond` is passed through a PHI node or something where `getPredicateAt` fails to use the range, but `getConstantRangeAtUse` succeeds?
Actually, `getPredicateAt` calls `LVI->getPredicateAt`, which uses the same underlying `LazyValueInfoCache`.
However, `getConstantRangeAtUse` might be more precise in some cases, or vice versa.
Wait, what if the switch has duplicate cases? LangRef says "The table is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries." So no duplicate cases.
What if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are NOT in the constant range?
Let's look at the patch again:
```cpp
      ConstantRange CR = LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse(I->getOperandUse(0),
                                                    /*UndefAllowed*/ false);
      // The default dest is unreachable if all cases are covered.
      if (!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)) {
```

Cases are `0` and `2`.
`ReachableCaseCount` is 2.
`CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is `false`.
So it thinks all cases are covered and default is unreachable.

Can `cond` be `1`? Yes, `CR` is `[0, 2)`, so `cond` can be `0` or `1`.
Why would case `2` be kept? If `LVI->getPredicateAt(ICMP_EQ, cond, 2)` returns `Unknown` instead of `False`.
Can `LVI->getPredicateAt` return `Unknown` when `CR` is `[0, 2)`?
If `LVI->getPredicateAt` is less precise than `getConstantRangeAtUse`, yes!
For example, if `cond` is an argument, and we have an assumption `assume(cond < 2)`.
`getConstantRangeAtUse` might use the assumption and return `[0, 2)`.
`getPredicateAt` might also use the assumption.
Is there any case where `getConstantRangeAtUse` is MORE precise than `getPredicateAt`?
Yes, `getConstantRangeAtUse` was recently added or modified to be more precise in some contexts, or maybe `getPredicateAt` doesn't use `UseBlockValue=true` properly in all sub-queries.
Wait, `getPredicateAt` is called with `UseBlockValue=true`.
Let's write a test where `CR` has size 2, cases are 0 and 2, and we trick LVI into keeping case 2 but returning `[0, 2)` for CR.",
      "The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior."
    ],
    [
      "Opaque Pointers / ptrtoint",
      "Switch on ptrtoint.",
      "If the switch condition is a `ptrtoint` of a pointer, LVI might have some range for it. If the range size is small, but cases are outside.",
      "The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior."
    ],
    [
      "ConstantRange Size Overflow",
      "Switch with empty constant range.",
      "If the constant range is full (e.g., `[0, 0)` for `i32`, which represents the full set in some contexts, wait, full set is `isFullSet()`). `isSizeLargerThan` for a full `i32` range and `ReachableCaseCount = 2`.
Wait, `ConstantRange::isSizeLargerThan(unsigned N)`:
If `CR` is full, its size is `1 << 32`. `isSizeLargerThan(2)` would be `true`.
What if `CR` is empty? Size is 0. `isSizeLargerThan(2)` is `false`.
If `CR` is empty, it means the switch is unreachable. Replacing default with unreachable is fine.",
      "The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior."
    ]
  ],
  "thoughts": "CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Use specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

1.  **Fix Understanding**:
    The patch aims to optimize `switch` instructions by checking if the default case is reachable. It does this by counting the number of reachable cases (`ReachableCaseCount`). If the size of the constant range of the switch condition (`CR`) is less than or equal to `ReachableCaseCount`, it assumes that all possible values in the constant range are covered by the cases, and therefore the default case is unreachable. It then replaces the default destination with an `unreachable` instruction.

2.  **Formal Semantic Modeling**:
    *   **Before**: The default case is left as is, unless all cases are explicitly handled or other optimizations apply.
    *   **After**: If `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)`, the default case is replaced with an `unreachable` block.
    *   **Condition for difference**: This differs when `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)` is true, but the default case is actually reachable.
    *   **Concrete instantiation**: Suppose the switch condition `x` has a constant range `CR = [0, 2)` (size 2, containing 0 and 1). The switch has cases `0` and `2`.
        If `LVI->getPredicateAt(x == 2)` returns `Unknown` (meaning it fails to prove it's false), the case `2` is kept.
        `ReachableCaseCount` becomes 2 (for cases `0` and `2`).
        `CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is `false` (since size is 2, which is not larger than 2).
        The optimization triggers and marks the default case as unreachable.
        However, if `x` is `1` (which is in `CR`), it should go to the default case. But the default case is now `unreachable`, causing undefined behavior!

3.  **Assumptions Identified**:
    *   The patch assumes that if `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getSize()`, then the reachable cases *must* cover all values in `CR`.
    *   This assumption relies on the precondition that *all* reachable cases are strictly within `CR`. If there are cases outside `CR` that were not eliminated (because `LVI->getPredicateAt` was not powerful enough to prove them false, or due to LVI cache inconsistencies), they will artificially inflate `ReachableCaseCount`.

4.  **Potential Cases to Break Assumptions**:
    *   We need a scenario where `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` is more precise than `LVI->getPredicateAt`, or where they yield different results such that a case outside the range is not eliminated.
    *   For example, `getConstantRangeAtUse` might use context-sensitive information (like `UseBlockValue=true` equivalent, though it doesn't take a block, it takes a Use). Wait, `getConstantRangeAtUse` takes a `Use`, which provides context. `getPredicateAt` takes a context instruction `I` and `UseBlockValue=true`.
    *   What if `CR` is calculated using some range metadata or assume that `getPredicateAt` misses?
    *   Or what if `CR` is `[0, 2)` and cases are `0` and `2`. If `getPredicateAt` for `x == 2` returns `Unknown`.
    *   Actually, `LVI` is known to sometimes have inconsistencies between `getPredicateAt` and `getConstantRange`.
    *   Another way: `ReachableCaseCount` counts the number of cases. What if there are multiple cases with the same value? LangRef says "The table is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries." So this is not possible.
    *   What if the switch condition is a `select` or `phi` where `getConstantRangeAtUse` can bound it, but `getPredicateAt` fails?
    *   Let's construct a test strategy based on this discrepancy. If we can make `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getSize()` while the cases do not cover `CR`, we expose the bug.

5.  **Test Strategies**:
    *   Strategy 1: Create a switch with a condition that has a known small range (e.g., `[0, 2)`). Add cases `0` and `2`. Obfuscate the condition so `getPredicateAt` cannot prove `cond == 2` is false, but `getConstantRangeAtUse` can still determine the range is `[0, 2)`.
    *   Strategy 2: Use `assume` to restrict the range of a value to `[0, 2)`. Switch on this value with cases `0` and `2`.
    *   Strategy 3: Use a `phi` node where one incoming value is `0` and the other is `1`. Switch on the `phi` with cases `0` and `2`.
    *   Strategy 4: Use `and x, 1` to restrict range to `[0, 2)`. Switch on it with cases `0` and `2`. (Though `getPredicateAt` might easily fold this).
    *   Strategy 5: Use `urem x, 2` to restrict range to `[0, 2)`. Switch on it with cases `0` and `2`.

    Let's refine Strategy 1/2/3. The core issue is the logical flaw: `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getSize()` does NOT imply `CR` is fully covered, unless we are guaranteed that all `ReachableCaseCount` cases are *inside* `CR`. Since `getPredicateAt` and `getConstantRangeAtUse` are different API calls, they might have different precision, leading to cases outside `CR` being counted. This is a classic LLVM pass bug where two different analysis queries are assumed to be perfectly consistent."
}

# Phase 2: Generate Verified Test Cases

You have proposed the following test strategies in Phase 1:

[{'name': 'Reachable Default Case with Multiple Reachable Cases', 'target': 'A switch instruction where the condition has a known constant range, but there are many cases, some of which are outside the constant range but LVI fails to prove them false, and the cases inside the constant range do not cover the entire constant range.', 'rationale': 'The patch checks if `ReachableCaseCount > 1` and if the constant range size is not larger than `ReachableCaseCount`. If so, it replaces the default destination with an unreachable block. However, if there are multiple reachable cases but they do not cover the entire constant range, the default case might still be reachable. The condition `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)` might be satisfied if the constant range size is exactly equal to `ReachableCaseCount`, but the cases might not exactly match the values in the constant range. Wait, `CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)` checks if the number of elements in the constant range is strictly greater than `ReachableCaseCount`. If it\'s not, it means the number of elements in the constant range is less than or equal to `ReachableCaseCount`. If the constant range has `N` elements and there are `M` reachable cases, and `N <= M`, does it mean all elements in the constant range are covered by the cases? Not necessarily. What if multiple cases have the same value? The LLVM Language Reference says "The table is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries." So all cases have distinct values. If the constant range has `N` elements and there are `M` distinct reachable cases, and `N <= M`, and all cases are within the constant range? Wait, the cases might not be within the constant range. If a case is outside the constant range, `LVI->getPredicateAt` would return `LazyValueInfo::False` and the case would be removed. So all remaining cases must overlap with the constant range. Since they are distinct integers, and there are `M` of them, and the constant range only contains `N` integers, and `N <= M`, it must be that `N = M` and the cases exactly cover the constant range. Wait, what if the constant range is not a single contiguous range, but `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` returns a bounding range? If the bounding range has size `N`, and there are `M` cases, `N <= M` implies the bounding range is exactly covered.\nWait, what if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are NOT in the constant range?\nLet\'s trace:\n```cpp\n    for (auto CI = SI->case_begin(), CE = SI->case_end(); CI != CE;) {\n      ConstantInt *Case = CI->getCaseValue();\n      LazyValueInfo::Tristate State =\n          LVI->getPredicateAt(CmpInst::ICMP_EQ, Cond, Case, I,\n                              /* UseBlockValue */ true);\n\n      if (State == LazyValueInfo::False) {\n        // removed\n        continue;\n      }\n      if (State == LazyValueInfo::True) {\n        // replaced with unconditional branch\n        break;\n      }\n      ++CI;\n      ++ReachableCaseCount;\n    }\n```\nIf `State` is `LazyValueInfo::Unknown`, the case is kept and `ReachableCaseCount` is incremented.\nCould there be a case where `State` is `Unknown` but the case value is actually outside the constant range?\n`LVI->getPredicateAt` might return `Unknown` even if the value is outside the constant range returned by `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse`, because they might use different analysis depths or context. However, usually `getPredicateAt` is at least as precise as `getConstantRangeAtUse`.\nBut what if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are unreachable, but LVI couldn\'t prove it? Then `ReachableCaseCount` could be larger than the actual number of reachable cases.\nIf `ReachableCaseCount >= CR.getUpper() - CR.getLower()`, the patch assumes the default case is unreachable.\nIs it possible that `ReachableCaseCount` is large because of some cases outside the constant range, and the cases INSIDE the constant range do NOT cover the whole constant range?\nYes! If `LVI->getPredicateAt` returns `Unknown` for a case outside the constant range (e.g., due to LVI limitations or different context), that case is counted in `ReachableCaseCount`. Then `ReachableCaseCount` could be >= `CR.getSetSize()`. But the cases inside `CR` might not cover all values in `CR`. Thus, there is a value in `CR` that is not covered by any case, so the default case is reachable. But the patch replaces it with `unreachable`!', 'expected_issue': 'The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior when the default case is actually reachable.'}, {'name': 'Constant Range with Undef/Poison', 'target': 'Switch instruction with undef/poison condition.', 'rationale': 'The patch uses `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse(I->getOperandUse(0), /*UndefAllowed*/ false)`. If the switch condition can be `undef` or `poison`, the constant range might not include `undef` or `poison`. Wait, if the condition is `undef` or `poison`, the `switch` instruction has undefined behavior anyway (as per LangRef: "If \'value\' is poison or undef, this instruction has undefined behavior."). So replacing the default dest with `unreachable` is valid if the only way to reach it is if the condition is `undef`/`poison`.', 'expected_issue': 'The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior.'}, {'name': 'Switch Condition is a Select with Unreachable Cases', 'target': "A switch instruction where the condition's constant range is small, but some cases outside the range are not folded by `getPredicateAt`, causing `ReachableCaseCount` to be >= `CR.getSize()`, while some values in `CR` are not covered by the cases.", 'rationale': 'Consider a switch condition `cond = select i1 %cmp, i32 %a, i32 %b`. If the constant range of `cond` is small, but there are many cases. Wait, the issue is whether `ReachableCaseCount` can be larger than the constant range size, while the cases don\'t cover the constant range.\nLet\'s construct a case where `LVI->getPredicateAt` returns `Unknown` for a case outside the constant range, but `LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` returns a small range.\n`LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse` uses `ValueLatticeElement`.\nIs it possible that `CR` is small, but `ReachableCaseCount` is large?\nSuppose `cond` is `and i32 %x, 3`. The constant range is `[0, 4)`. Size is 4.\nSuppose the switch has cases: 0, 1, 4, 5, 6.\nWill `LVI->getPredicateAt(ICMP_EQ, cond, 4)` return `False`?\n`cond` is `and i32 %x, 3`. LVI knows its range is `[0, 4)`. So `cond == 4` is `False`.\nWhat if `cond` is passed through a PHI node or something where `getPredicateAt` fails to use the range, but `getConstantRangeAtUse` succeeds?\nActually, `getPredicateAt` calls `LVI->getPredicateAt`, which uses the same underlying `LazyValueInfoCache`.\nHowever, `getConstantRangeAtUse` might be more precise in some cases, or vice versa.\nWait, what if the switch has duplicate cases? LangRef says "The table is not allowed to contain duplicate constant entries." So no duplicate cases.\nWhat if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are NOT in the constant range?\nLet\'s look at the patch again:\n```cpp\n      ConstantRange CR = LVI->getConstantRangeAtUse(I->getOperandUse(0),\n                                                    /*UndefAllowed*/ false);\n      // The default dest is unreachable if all cases are covered.\n      if (!CR.isSizeLargerThan(ReachableCaseCount)) {\n```\nIf `CR` has size 2 (e.g., `[0, 2)`).\nCases are `0` and `2`.\n`ReachableCaseCount` is 2.\n`CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is `false`.\nSo it thinks all cases are covered and default is unreachable.\nBut the cases are `0` and `2`. The value `1` is in `CR`, but not in the cases!\nIf `cond` can be `1`, it will go to the default case. But the default case is replaced with `unreachable`!\nCan `cond` be `1`? Yes, `CR` is `[0, 2)`, so `cond` can be `0` or `1`.\nWhy would case `2` be kept? If `LVI->getPredicateAt(ICMP_EQ, cond, 2)` returns `Unknown` instead of `False`.\nCan `LVI->getPredicateAt` return `Unknown` when `CR` is `[0, 2)`?\nIf `LVI->getPredicateAt` is less precise than `getConstantRangeAtUse`, yes!\nFor example, if `cond` is an argument, and we have an assumption `assume(cond < 2)`.\n`getConstantRangeAtUse` might use the assumption and return `[0, 2)`.\n`getPredicateAt` might also use the assumption.\nIs there any case where `getConstantRangeAtUse` is MORE precise than `getPredicateAt`?\nYes, `getConstantRangeAtUse` was recently added or modified to be more precise in some contexts, or maybe `getPredicateAt` doesn\'t use `UseBlockValue=true` properly in all sub-queries.\nWait, `getPredicateAt` is called with `UseBlockValue=true`.\nLet\'s write a test where `CR` has size 2, cases are 0 and 2, and we trick LVI into keeping case 2 but returning `[0, 2)` for CR.', 'expected_issue': 'The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior.'}, {'name': 'Opaque Pointers / ptrtoint', 'target': 'Switch on ptrtoint.', 'rationale': 'If the switch condition is a `ptrtoint` of a pointer, LVI might have some range for it. Size is 0. `isSizeLargerThan(2)` is `false`.\nIf `CR` is empty, it means the switch is unreachable. Replacing default with unreachable is fine.', 'expected_issue': 'The default destination is incorrectly replaced with an unreachable block, leading to undefined behavior.'}]

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Prioritize Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Due to context length and round limit, you should prioritize test cases that are more relevant to the identified issues.

### Step 2: Understand the Test Case
- Analyze the selected test case to understand its structure, input characteristics, and what it is testing.
- Call the `verify` tool to see how the LLVM IR code in the test case is transformed by the optimization pass. This can help you understand the semantics of the test case and how it relates to the fix.

### Step 3: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 4: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 5: Analyze Results and Refine
- If the `verify` or `difftest` fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases. Check **Guidelines for Refining Test Cases** below for suggestions on how to refine test cases based on verification results.

### Step 6: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

Repeat the above steps for each test case until all test cases in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1. Meanwhile, avoid stucking on a test case for too long. If you have tried multiple mutations and still cannot find any issues, move on to other test cases or strategies.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

arguments: {"action":"get","index":1}

{
  "test_name": "switch_range",
  "test_body": "define i32 @switch_range(i32 %cond) {
entry:
  %s = urem i32 %cond, 3
  %s1 = add i32 %s, 1
  switch i32 %s1, label %unreachable [
    i32 1, label %exit1
    i32 2, label %exit2
    i32 3, label %exit1
  ]

exit1:                                            ; preds = %entry, %entry
  ret i32 1

exit2:                                            ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 2

unreachable:                                      ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 0
}
",

}

arguments:
{"test_index":1,"args":"-S -passes=correlated-propagation","thoughts":"CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Use specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

I will mutate `switch_range` to test the "Reachable Default Case with Multiple Reachable Cases" strategy.
The strategy requires a switch condition with a known constant range, but some cases outside the range are not folded by `getPredicateAt`, causing `ReachableCaseCount` to be >= `CR.getSize()`, while some values in `CR` are not covered by the cases.
Let's construct:
`%s = urem i32 %cond, 2` -> range `[0, 2)`
`%s1 = add i32 %s, 1` -> range `[1, 3)` (size 2)
Cases: `1` and `3`.
`ReachableCaseCount` will be 2.
`CR.getSize()` is 2.
`!CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is true.
So the default case will be replaced with `unreachable`.
But if `%cond` is 1, `%s` is 1, `%s1` is 2.
Case `2` is not in the switch, so it goes to the default case.
But the default case is replaced with `unreachable`!
Let's test this with `difftest`.","call_instr":"call i32 @switch_range(i32 0)","covered_strategy":"Reachable Default Case with Multiple Reachable Cases","action":"test","orig_ir":"define i32 @switch_range(i32 %cond) {

    i32 3, label %exit2
  ]

exit1:

exit2:
  ret i32 2

unreachable:
  ret i32 0
}
"}

```","thoughts":"CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 1: Use specific tools.
CRITICAL INSTRUCTION 2: List related tools.

The switch was simplified to a branch because there was only one reachable case (case 1). Case 3 was removed because `getPredicateAt` proved it false.
Wait, `getPredicateAt` proved case 3 false!
If `getPredicateAt` proves it false, it is removed, and `ReachableCaseCount` is not incremented.
We need a case where `getPredicateAt` CANNOT prove it false, but `getConstantRangeAtUse` CAN bound the range.
How can we make `getPredicateAt` fail while `getConstantRangeAtUse` succeeds?
`getPredicateAt` uses `LVI->getPredicateAt`.
Let's try using `select` or `phi` or `assume`.
Wait, `getPredicateAt` for `ICMP_EQ` uses `LVI->getPredicateAt`.
If we use `assume(cond < 2)`, `getConstantRangeAtUse` might use it.
Let's try `assume`.","args":"-S -passes=correlated-propagation"}

If we use `UndefAllowed=false`, does it change the range of `and i32 %x, 1`? No, it's still `[0, 2)`.
What if the value is `select i1 %cond, i32 0, i32 2`? Range is `[0, 3)`.
What if we have a `phi` node?
Is there any case where `getConstantRangeAtUse(..., false)` returns a smaller range than `getPredicateAt`?
Yes, if `getPredicateAt` uses `getConstantRange(..., true)` internally, and the `true` makes the range larger.
For example, if a `phi` node has an `undef` incoming value.
`%phi = phi i32 [ 0, %bb1 ], [ undef, %bb2 ]`
`getConstantRange(..., true)` must include `undef`, so it might return `full-set`.
`getConstantRange(..., false)` can ignore the `undef` incoming value, so it returns `[0, 1)`.
Let's test this!
If `%phi` is `[0, 1)` with `UndefAllowed=false`. Size is 1.
Cases are `0` and `2`.
`getPredicateAt` uses `full-set`, so it returns `Unknown` for case `2`.
`ReachableCaseCount` will be 2 (cases 0 and 2).
`CR` is `[0, 1)` (size 1).
`CR.getSize()` is 1. `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is true.
So the default case is replaced with `unreachable`.
But wait, if `%phi` can be `undef`, the switch has undefined behavior anyway!
"If 'value' is poison or undef, this instruction has undefined behavior."
So replacing the default case with `unreachable` is VALID if the only way to reach it is when `%phi` is `undef`.
Wait, what if `%phi` is NOT `undef`, but the default case is reached?
If `%phi` is not `undef`, it MUST be `0`.
If it is `0`, it goes to case `0`.
So the default case is NEVER reached unless `%phi` is `undef`.
So replacing it with `unreachable` is STILL VALID!

Wait, what if `getConstantRangeAtUse` is more precise because it uses the `Use` context?
For example, if the `SwitchInst` is inside a block where `assume(cond < 2)` dominates the `SwitchInst`, but does NOT dominate the definition of `cond`.
`getConstantRangeAtUse` will use the `assume` because it takes the `Use` in the `SwitchInst`.
`getPredicateAt` takes the `SwitchInst` as the context instruction. It should ALSO use the `assume`.
So they should be equally precise.

What if `getPredicateAt` is LESS precise because of some cache invalidation or depth limit?
If `getPredicateAt` hits a depth limit, it returns `Unknown`.
If `getConstantRangeAtUse` does NOT hit a depth limit, it returns `[0, 2)`.
Then `ReachableCaseCount` will include cases outside `[0, 2)`.
Then `CR.getSize() <= ReachableCaseCount` will be true.
And the default case will be replaced with `unreachable`.
But the cases inside `[0, 2)` might NOT cover `[0, 2)`!
For example, `CR` is `[0, 2)` (size 2).
Cases are `0` and `3`.
`getPredicateAt` hits depth limit for case `3`, returns `Unknown`.
`ReachableCaseCount` is 2.
`CR.getSize()` is 2. `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is true.
So default case is replaced with `unreachable`.
But case `1` is NOT covered!
If `cond` is `1`, it goes to default case. But default case is `unreachable`!
This is a BUG!
How to make `getPredicateAt` hit a depth limit while `getConstantRangeAtUse` does not?
Or how to make `getPredicateAt` fail for some other reason?
Let's look at `LazyValueInfo::getPredicateAt`.
It calls `getPredicateAt` which calls `solveBlockValue`.
`getConstantRangeAtUse` calls `getConstantRange` which calls `solveBlockValue`.
They use the SAME underlying logic!
Is there ANY difference?
`getPredicateAt` for `ICMP_EQ` with a constant `C` checks if `C` is in the range returned by `getConstantRange`.
Wait, `getPredicateAt` calls `getConstantRange`!
If `getPredicateAt` calls `getConstantRange`, then they MUST return the SAME range!
Let's check `LazyValueInfo.cpp`:
```cpp
LazyValueInfo::Tristate LazyValueInfo::getPredicateAt(unsigned P, Value *LHS,
                                                      Constant *RHS,
                                                      Instruction *CxtI,
                                                      bool UseBlockValue) {
  ...
  ConstantRange LHS_CR = getConstantRange(LHS, CxtI, UndefAllowed);
  ...
  return LHS_CR.icmp(P, RHS_CR);
}
```
If `getPredicateAt` calls `getConstantRange`, then `LHS_CR` is EXACTLY the same as `CR` returned by `getConstantRangeAtUse`!
Wait, `getConstantRangeAtUse` calls `getConstantRange` with `UndefAllowed=false`.
`getPredicateAt` calls `getConstantRange` with `UndefAllowed=true` (or whatever the default is).
If `UndefAllowed=true` returns a LARGER range than `UndefAllowed=false`, then `getPredicateAt` might return `Unknown` for a case outside the `UndefAllowed=false` range!
Let's verify this!
If `UndefAllowed=true` returns `full-set`, and `UndefAllowed=false` returns `[0, 2)`.

`ReachableCaseCount` is 2 (cases 0 and 3).

If `cond` is `1`, it goes to default case. But default case is `unreachable`!

If `UndefAllowed=false` returns `[0, 2)`, it means "if `cond` is not undef/poison, it must be in `[0, 2)`".
So if `cond` is not undef/poison, it can be `0` or `1`.
If it is `1`, it goes to default case.
But default case is `unreachable`!

When does `UndefAllowed=false` return a smaller range?
Let's look at `LazyValueInfo::getConstantRange`.
It uses `ValueLatticeElement`.
If a value is `undef`, its lattice element is `unknown` or `undef`.
If we have a `phi` node with an `undef` incoming value:

With `UndefAllowed=true`, the range must include `undef`, which means it might be `full-set` or `[0, 2)` depending on how `undef` is handled.
Actually, `ValueLatticeElement` handles `undef` by merging it with the other values.
Wait, `ValueLatticeElement::mergeIn` with `undef` might just ignore it if `UndefAllowed=false`?
No, `ValueLatticeElement` doesn't know about `UndefAllowed`.
`UndefAllowed` is handled in `getConstantRange`:
```cpp
ConstantRange LazyValueInfo::getConstantRange(Value *V, Instruction *CxtI,
                                              bool UndefAllowed) {
  ValueLatticeElement Result = getBBLatticeValue(V, CxtI->getParent(), CxtI);
  if (Result.isUnknown()) return ConstantRange::getFull(V->getType()->getScalarSizeInBits());
  if (Result.isConstantRange()) {
    ConstantRange CR = Result.getConstantRange();
    if (UndefAllowed && Result.isConstantRangeIncludingUndef())

    return CR;
  }
  ...
}
```

This means `getPredicateAt` proved `%sel == 3` is false, and also `%sel == 0` is true.

Let's rethink.
Is there ANY case where `CR.getSize() <= ReachableCaseCount`, but the cases DO NOT cover `CR`?
This can ONLY happen if `ReachableCaseCount` includes cases that are OUTSIDE `CR`.
How can a case be outside `CR` but `getPredicateAt` returns `Unknown` or `True`?
This requires `getPredicateAt` to use a LARGER range than `getConstantRangeAtUse`.
Why would `getPredicateAt` use a larger range?
Because `getConstantRangeAtUse` is called with `/*UndefAllowed*/ false`.
`getPredicateAt` might be called with `UndefAllowed=true` internally?
Let's check `LVI->getPredicateAt`. It doesn't take `UndefAllowed`. It assumes `UndefAllowed=true` to be safe, or it handles undef conservatively.
If `getConstantRangeAtUse` with `UndefAllowed=false` returns a SMALLER range than with `UndefAllowed=true`.
When does `UndefAllowed=false` return a smaller range?
If the value is `undef`, the range is empty. But if it's not `undef`, is the range smaller?
Wait, `getConstantRangeAtUse` with `UndefAllowed=false` can return a smaller range if it knows the value cannot be `undef`? No, if `UndefAllowed=false`, it means we don't care about the `undef` case (we can assume it's not `undef`, or if it is, it's UB).
Actually, `getConstantRangeAtUse` with `UndefAllowed=false` means "return the range of the value, assuming it is not undef/poison".
If the value is `poison`, the range can be anything (e.g., empty).
If the value is `and i32 %x, 1`. Range is `[0, 2)`.
If `%x` is `poison`, `and` is `poison`.
If we use `UndefAllowed=false`, does it change the range of `and i32 %x, 1`? No, it's still `[0, 2)`.
What if the value is `select i1 %cond, i32 0, i32 2`? Range is `[0, 3)`.
What if we have a `phi` node?
Is there any case where `getConstantRangeAtUse(..., false)` returns a smaller range than `getPredicateAt`?
Yes, if `getPredicateAt` uses `getConstantRange(..., true)` internally, and the `true` makes the range larger.
For example, if a `phi` node has an `undef` incoming value.
`%phi = phi i32 [ 0, %bb1 ], [ undef, %bb2 ]`
`getConstantRange(..., true)` must include `undef`, so it might return `full-set`.
`getConstantRange(..., false)` can ignore the `undef` incoming value, so it returns `[0, 1)`.
Let's test this!
If `%phi` is `[0, 1)` with `UndefAllowed=false`. Size is 1.
Cases are `0` and `2`.
`getPredicateAt` uses `full-set`, so it returns `Unknown` for case `2`.
`ReachableCaseCount` will be 2 (cases 0 and 2).
`CR` is `[0, 1)` (size 1).
`CR.getSize()` is 1. `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is true.
So the default case is replaced with `unreachable`.
But wait, if `%phi` can be `undef`, the switch has undefined behavior anyway!
"If 'value' is poison or undef, this instruction has undefined behavior."
So replacing the default case with `unreachable` is VALID if the only way to reach it is when `%phi` is `undef`.
Wait, what if `%phi` is NOT `undef`, but the default case is reached?
If `%phi` is not `undef`, it MUST be `0`.
If it is `0`, it goes to case `0`.
So the default case is NEVER reached unless `%phi` is `undef`.
So replacing it with `unreachable` is STILL VALID!

Wait, what if `%phi` is `phi i32 [ 0, %bb1 ], [ 1, %bb2 ], [ undef, %bb3 ]`?
`CR` will be `[0, 2)` (size 2).
Cases: `0` and `3`.
`getPredicateAt` will use `full-set`, so case `3` is kept.
`ReachableCaseCount` is 2.
`CR.getSize()` is 2. `!CR.isSizeLargerThan(2)` is true.
Default case is replaced with `unreachable`.
But if `%phi` is `1` (which is possible, from `%bb2`), it goes to the default case!
But the default case is replaced with `unreachable`!
This is a BUG!
Because `%phi` CAN be `1`, and `1` is NOT `undef`.
So the switch does NOT have undefined behavior when `%phi` is `1`.
But it will hit `unreachable`!
